elements.sent-2411-008: Acknowledgements As I write this Acknowledgement, my defence is exactly a month away.
elements.sent-2411-009: I delayed writing this, both intentionally and unintentionally.
elements.sent-2411-010: I wanted to ensure I didn’t miss the chance to thank anyone who contributed to this thesis, and, admittedly, I am also habitually behind schedule.
elements.sent-2411-011: Looking back, I realise that every person I’ve met has shaped this journey.
elements.sent-2411-012: Pursuing a PhD in the Netherlands has been a remarkable learning experience in both mathematics and life, and I would like to thank all of you through these brief stories and lessons we shared.
elements.sent-2411-013: Since childhood, I’ve always been drawn to research.
elements.sent-2411-014: For PhD applications abroad, I needed recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-015: One of my seniors, Sohom Bhattacharya (Sohom B), advised me to do a summer project with Prof.
elements.sent-2411-017: Krishnapur introduced me to the fascinating world of random walks and trees.
elements.sent-2411-018: Looking back, this was the beginning of my PhD journey in probability theory.
elements.sent-2411-019: During my Master’s degree at the Indian Statistical Institute (ISI) in Kolkata, I was actively seeking someone who could provide a recommendation for my PhD applications.
elements.sent-2411-020: I admit that my initial reason for meeting Rajat daa was this purpose alone, but when he suggested I attend his summer course on random graphs, I was immediately captivated.
elements.sent-2411-021: My first impression was, “It’s tough maths, but not tougher than Statistics,” and it was then that I decided to pursue Probability as a field of research.
elements.sent-2411-022: At that time, I had only a vague sense of the research world and a somewhat naïve vision of my future.
elements.sent-2411-023: Toward the end of my first year, I approached Rajat daa for a Master’s project, specifically in random walks.
elements.sent-2411-024: He entrusted me with a project close to his heart, and I enjoyed working with my collaborators Dr. Alessandra Cipriani and Dr. Biltu Dan.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, I soon encountered my first reality check: preparing for the GRE and TOEFL, necessary for US PhD applications.
elements.sent-2411-026: Despite multiple attempts, I struggled with these exams, and with the onset of COVID, all US universities imposed a hiring freeze.
elements.sent-2411-027: With rejection letters piling up, I felt stranded with no PhD offers.
elements.sent-2411-028: I turned again to Rajat daa, who encouraged me to apply for open position at the NETWORKS COFUND PhD programme.
elements.sent-2411-029: He gave me invaluable support and recommendations.
elements.sent-2411-030: I was extremely nervous during my interview with NETWORKS COFUND, where I met Remco (van der Hofstad) and Frank (den Hollander).
elements.sent-2411-031: I was struck by how approachable these eminent professors were and later realised it is their genuine nature to bring out the best in their students.
elements.sent-2411-032: After my selection at NETWORKS COFUND, I began to feel that perhaps this was where I was meant to be, and that all those rejections had led me to the right place.
elements.sent-2411-033: Just as my career was taking off, my father (Baba) was diagnosed with terminal-stage cancer.
elements.sent-2411-034: He had sparked my interest in mathematics and research during my childhood, but by the time I started my research journey, he was nearing the end.
elements.sent-2411-035: Despite his condition, he urged me to go to the Netherlands to join the programme, while my mother (Maa) accompanied him to Mumbai for treatment.
elements.sent-2411-036: The doctors there gave him a median life expectancy of just six months, meaning he likely wouldn’t live to see me complete my PhD, a dream we had shared.
elements.sent-2411-037: Six months later, when he passed away, the entire SPOR community was there to support me.
elements.sent-2411-038: Upon returning from India, I received a heartfelt card from everyone in SPOR, and that’s when this place truly began to feel like family.
elements.sent-2411-039: The support of Suman daa, Manish, Arpan daa, and Neeladri was invaluable during those times.
elements.sent-2411-040: Although I did my theoretical cooking course before coming to the Netherlands, I actually cooked for the first time here, with Arpan daa as my guinea pig.
elements.sent-2411-041: My passion for food soon made cooking a favourite pastime, and Arpan daa's `critical' feedback motivated me to keep experimenting.
elements.sent-2411-042: In the first few months, these friends were my only companions, but as lockdowns eased, I met more fantastic people in the department, like Joost, Martijn, and Zsolt.
elements.sent-2411-043: Since Joost also works on preferential attachment models, we share a unique bond.
elements.sent-2411-044: Together, we went on long bike rides, travelled to Paris for a conference, and I presented my work there for the first time—masks on and all.
elements.sent-2411-045: It was daunting but made easier by their support.
elements.sent-2411-046: I also fondly remember Friday evenings, where I had my first drinks, spurred on by this fantastic group under the able leadership of Pim (van der Hoorn) and Joost.
elements.sent-2411-047: When the restrictions were lifted, I met my other officemates, Samaneh and Marta.
elements.sent-2411-048: I’m grateful to Samaneh for being my tea partner — our tea-time conversations ranged from international politics to the similarities between Hindi and Urdu.
elements.sent-2411-049: Our `dart-throw' competitions, even during times of intense pressure, were both spirited and, surprisingly, relaxing.
elements.sent-2411-050: This was also when I joined a fantastic teaching team for a unique course with Alberto, Sandra, Tim, Martijn, and Hans.
elements.sent-2411-051: Special thanks to Tim, Sandra, and Hans for sparing me the task of learning SAS! Over three years, Martijn was succeeded by Beno\^{it and Stan, but they all kindly allowed me to focus on the theoretical aspects of the course.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Mike’s course was also invaluable, helping me revise tools like coupling and stochastic dominance, which I used frequently during my PhD.
elements.sent-2411-053: Winning arguments with Manish wouldn’t have been as entertaining without Tom, Fatuma, and Mark.
elements.sent-2411-054: Your office became my go-to spot for trash-talking.
elements.sent-2411-055: Special thanks to Tom for regularly bringing brownies and cakes, which were my afternoon snacks most of the time.
elements.sent-2411-056: Haodong is someone I can always rely on for finding loopholes in proofs or identifying counterintuitive examples.
elements.sent-2411-057: I hope we can get at least one research paper from our work at ASML.
elements.sent-2411-058: I’ve rarely seen anyone as enthusiastic as Nelly when tackling new challenges.
elements.sent-2411-059: I’m also impressed by her simple yet effective technique for remembering family relations in Bengali—a perspective I hadn’t thought of before.
elements.sent-2411-060: Her teaching philosophy inspires me, and I aspire to be as dedicated a teacher while also excelling as a researcher.
elements.sent-2411-061: I feel fortunate to have Pranab daa in the neighbouring office.
elements.sent-2411-062: Our conversations in Bengali about teaching, the Dutch healthcare system, elections, Durga Puja, thesis work, and late-night office were always both refreshing and enjoyable.
elements.sent-2411-063: Remco, words cannot express my gratitude for standing by me at every stage of these four years of my PhD.
elements.sent-2411-064: You took the time and effort to clarify every single doubt, even the ones that felt stupid (though you always say, ``No doubts are stupid!").
elements.sent-2411-065: I’ve learned countless life lessons from you.
elements.sent-2411-066: Whenever I needed help, whether with maths or other issues, your door was always open.
elements.sent-2411-067: Our discussions—whether while biking, driving, or travelling by train—have consistently sparked fruitful ideas.
elements.sent-2411-068: Daylong conversations with Rajat daa also helped immensely, filling in gaps in my arguments and catching up on ISI gossips.
elements.sent-2411-069: Frank, although you weren’t my direct supervisor, you were nothing short of one to me.
elements.sent-2411-070: I have always benefited greatly from your advice and support.
elements.sent-2411-071: Next, I would like to thank my committee members; Bert Zwart, Peter M\"{orters, Maria Deijfen and the chair J.N.. Kok.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Thank you all for taking time out of your busy schedule to read the thesis, join the defence and preparing questions.
elements.sent-2411-073: Although I wasn’t regular at the EURANDOM lunch table, the conversations were often interesting, enriched by the insights of Purva, Mike, Connor, Stan, Sanne, Ellen, Ivo, Thomas, Ignachio (Nacho), Alexander, and Nelly.
elements.sent-2411-074: Special thanks go to Noela and Pim for organising events and activities for the Probability cluster.
elements.sent-2411-075: Although my interactions with Qiu, Isabel, and Kai-Chun, the newest members of the cluster, have been brief, I’ve enjoyed their company.
elements.sent-2411-076: Lorenzo, I trust you’ll lead the office with excellence.
elements.sent-2411-077: And Vinay, now that we’ve found a shared interest in card tricks and games, I think it’s time to make it a tradition! The NETWORKS training weeks were a unique experience for all affiliates.
elements.sent-2411-078: Although informally dubbed `NOTWORKS' weeks, we engaged in some serious activities—bowling, dart-throwing, card games, swimming, sauna, and of course, partying and lastly attending mini-courses.
elements.sent-2411-079: The `after \(11\)' parties at our secret hideouts are unforgettable, with Gianluca, Tim, Roshan, and Marta as the core club members.
elements.sent-2411-080: Marieke, I don’t know how you manage to remember every detail about each NETWORKS affiliate, but you do so effortlessly, and your support with travel-budget tracking and event organisation is unmatched.
elements.sent-2411-081: Attending conferences with our gang—Nandan, Federico, Daoyi, Twan, Oliver, and Manish—was a lot of fun.
elements.sent-2411-082: Although there were some consequences, I am glad that you didn't care at all about those.
elements.sent-2411-083: Highlights like walking to the geographic centre of the Netherlands, getting drunk and breaking glass, late-night chats outside the hotel in Lunteren, and our time in Saint Flour were made all the more memorable with friends like Riccardo, Lars, and Maike.
elements.sent-2411-084: I would like to express my deep gratitude for the invaluable support from Chantal, Astrid, Marianne, and Ellen, the secretaries of the SPOR cluster.
elements.sent-2411-085: During the organisation of the lecture series in 2023, Marianne and Marieke handled all administrative tasks seamlessly, ensuring the process went smoothly.
elements.sent-2411-086: Chantal and Astrid remain my go-to contacts for meeting scheduling, office-related matters, and arranging my defence ceremony.
elements.sent-2411-087: Despite your heavy workloads and the fact that I am not directly your PhD student, Sem, you provided valuable guidance whenever I needed it during the lecture series organisation.
elements.sent-2411-088: Thank you all for your exceptional support.
elements.sent-2411-089: Outside the exceptional workplace at TU/e, I was fortunate to be surrounded by a wonderful Indian community, including Suman daa, Sreetama di, Sampad, Manish, Arpan daa, Shivesh, Purva, Nitant, Sreehari, Bharti, Sanjana, Rajeshwari, and Neeladri.
elements.sent-2411-090: Our weekly market trips, birthday celebrations, and Holi and Diwali festivities, along with our Durga Puja excursions to Blixembosch, made me feel almost at home.
elements.sent-2411-091: Playing cricket on the field and in our living room at night, and staying up late with Arpan daa, Manish, Shivesh, Nitant, and Purva playing \(29\) and other card games, was an incredible amount of fun.
elements.sent-2411-092: Making important life decisions became easier thanks to the constant guidance from Suman daa, Nivedita di, and Sreetama di.
elements.sent-2411-093: Thanks to seniors, like Arnab daa (both Chowdhury and Chakraborty), Sandipan daa, Indranil daa, Arkajyoti daa, Shaswat daa, Mayukh daa, Tamal daa, Rajarshi daa, PPG, Chinmay daa, Deepan daa, Sagnik daa, Dipanjan daa, Aliva di, Shaswati di, Srijita di, Basundhara di, Tulip di, Suprita di and Agnishikha di, celebrating Durga Puja in the Netherlands felt just like home.
elements.sent-2411-094: I could not have reached this point without friends like Manaswini, Sayantan, and Sayan from ISI, Kolkata, who supported me through the intense curriculum.
elements.sent-2411-095: I fondly miss those good old ISI days, though they are somewhat compensated by our occasional evening texts and WhatsApp video calls.
elements.sent-2411-096: Although the Portugal plan with Sayan didn’t work out, I hope the Berkeley plan will be a success.
elements.sent-2411-097: This acknowledgement would be incomplete without expressing my heartfelt gratitude to Maa, Baba, Bhai (my brother), and Prasmita.
elements.sent-2411-098: Coming from a small city in India, my journey to the Indian Statistical Institute was far from easy.
elements.sent-2411-099: Baba envisioned this path and went beyond his capacity to equip me with the knowledge needed to succeed in those competitive exams.
elements.sent-2411-100: Maa ensured that everything at home was well under control while I was away.
elements.sent-2411-101: Without her presence, I could not have even contemplated leaving India when Baba was diagnosed with cancer.
elements.sent-2411-102: She has been the source of all my mental strength, teaching me how to stay calm and make decisions in challenging situations, a skill that greatly aided me during difficult phases of my PhD.
elements.sent-2411-103: When I left home, Bhai was still an immature boy, but when the situation demanded it, he demonstrated an extraordinary maturity far beyond his years.
elements.sent-2411-104: His unwavering support at home was unmatched, especially after Baba passed away.
elements.sent-2411-105: Prasmita, your constant support over the past 11 years through every challenge has been invaluable.
elements.sent-2411-106: You tolerated all my tantrums and stood by me in my difficult times as well as during moments of success.
elements.sent-2411-107: Without you, I would not have overcome those moments of self-doubt or questioned my own abilities.
elements.sent-2411-108: The four of you have been the pillars of support and inspiration throughout my journey.
elements.sent-2411-109: Without all of you, completing this work would not have been possible.
elements.sent-2411-110: From the Internet, social media, and telecommunications to biological systems, transportation infrastructures, and financial markets, networks provide a robust framework for understanding the complex interactions within and between different entities.
elements.sent-2411-111: Real-life networks exhibit fascinating structural properties that have profound implications for their functionality, resilience, and evolution.
elements.sent-2411-112: Empirical studies on real-life networks reveal that most of these networks {(a) grow with time; {(b) are small worlds, meaning that typical distances in the network are small; and {(c) have power-law degree sequences.}}}
elements.sent-2411-113: Real-life networks are composed of nodes (or vertices) and edges (or links) that connect these nodes.
elements.sent-2411-114: These networks can vary greatly in scale and complexity.
elements.sent-2411-115: For example, in a social network, nodes represent individuals or organizations, while edges represent social ties, such as friendships or collaborations.
elements.sent-2411-116: In a biological network, nodes might represent genes or proteins, and edges represent biochemical interactions.
elements.sent-2411-117: Despite the diversity of these systems, they often share common structural features that are critical to their behavior and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-118: To better understand and predict the behavior of real-life networks, researchers have turned to the study of random graphs.
elements.sent-2411-119: A random graph is a graph that is generated by some random process, and it serves as a probabilistic model for understanding the structural properties of networks.
elements.sent-2411-120: Among the various types of random graphs, sparse random graphs are of particular interest because they provide a more realistic model for large-scale networks where each node has only a few connections compared to the total number of vertices.
elements.sent-2411-121: A sparse random graph has a number of edges that is proportional to the number of vertices, rather than the square of the number of vertices, which is common in dense graphs.
elements.sent-2411-122: This sparsity reflects the reality of many networks where each vertex interacts with only a small fraction of other vertices, yet the overall network remains interconnected.
elements.paragraph-2411-003: Power-law random graphs: capturing real-world complexity
elements.sent-2411-123: Power-law random graphs represent a significant advancement in the modeling of real-life networks, particularly in capturing their degree distributions.
elements.sent-2411-124: Voitalov et al.\ showed in \cite{voitalov2019 that real-world scale-free networks are definitely not as rare as one would conclude based on the popular but unrealistic assumption that real-world data comes from power laws of pristine purity, void of noise and deviations, defying the concerns raised in \cite{BC19 .}}
elements.sent-2411-125: In a power-law distribution, the probability that a node has degree \( k \) follows the relation \begin{equation}P(k) = L(k)k^{-\tau}~,\end{equation} where \( \tau \) is a positive constant typically between $2$ and $3$, and \(L(k)\) is a slowly-varying function.
elements.sent-2411-126: This implies that most vertices have a small degree, while a few vertices, often called hubs, have a very high degree.
elements.sent-2411-127: These hubs play a critical role in the network’s structure and dynamics, making the network both resilient and vulnerable to targeted attacks.
elements.sent-2411-128: The power-law degree distribution is a defining characteristic of scale-free networks, a class of networks that includes many real-life examples, such as the Internet, social networks, and biological systems.
elements.sent-2411-129: The term scale-free refers to the fact that the network’s structural properties are similar across different scales.
elements.sent-2411-130: For instance, the same degree distribution pattern holds whether we examine the network at a local, regional, or global level.
elements.sent-2411-131: We are typically not only interested in networks, but also in their functionality.
elements.sent-2411-132: Indeed, we wish to know how quickly a disease or a rumor spreads, how products compete for the market in social networks, how damage restricts the network’s functionality, how people reach consensus when talking to one another, etc. Here we model networks using random graphs, while we model their functionality by stochastic processes on them.
elements.sent-2411-133: In recent decades, a tremendous body of work was developed discussing stochastic processes on random graphs \begin{center {Organisation of this chapter \end{center This chapter is organised into three sections, each laying the groundwork for the subsequent analysis of random graphs and stochastic processes on these graphs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-134: Following this, we introduce the affine preferential attachment model in a general form, highlighting its dynamic nature and relevance to the real-world networks.
elements.sent-2411-135: Section~\ref{chap:intro:sec:stoc-proc shifts focus to stochastic processes on graphs, with an emphasis on random graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-136: In this section, we introduce and explore two key processes: percolation and the Ising model.
elements.sent-2411-137: These processes are crucial for understanding the behaviour of complex networks and form a significant part of the analysis presented in this thesis.
elements.sent-2411-138: Section~\ref{chap:into:sec:tools-techniques summarises various tools and techniques used to study stochastic processes on random graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-139: We discuss the concept of local limits, branching processes, P\'{o lya urn representations, and the collapsing operator.}
elements.sent-2411-140: These tools are essential for approaching the challenges posed by the analysis of stochastic processes on preferential attachment models and other types of random graphs.
elements.sent-2411-141: This chapter provides a comprehensive introduction to the key concepts, models, and techniques that underpin the research presented in the thesis, setting the stage for the detailed discussions in the subsequent chapters.
elements.sent-2411-142: A graph $G = (V,E)$ consists of a vertex set $V$, and a set of edges $E\subset \{\{i,j\}: i,j\in V\}$ specifying the connections between different vertices.
elements.sent-2411-143: For a multigraph, $E$ is a multi-set possibly consisting of multiple-edges between vertices, as well as self-loops.
elements.sent-2411-145: A random graph model specifies a probability distribution over the space of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-146: We will consider $n$ vertices labeled by $[n]:=\{1,2,...,n\}$, which will serve as the vertex set of the random graph.
elements.sent-2411-147: We now discuss some classical static random graph models, and then move on to define the preferential attachment model in great generality.
elements.sent-2411-148: These graphs has a very rich, but still growing literature, and for detailed investigations of these models and recent developments, we refer the reader to \cite{vdH1,vdH2 .}
elements.sent-2411-149: One of the most well-known models for generating random graphs is the Erdős-Rényi (ER) model, introduced by Paul Erdős and Alfréd Rényi in \cite{ER59,ER60 .}
elements.sent-2411-150: The ER model is simple and elegant, providing a foundational framework for the study of random graphs.
elements.sent-2411-151: The ER model comes in two closely related versions:.
elements.sent-2411-152: G(n, p) model: In this version, the graph \( G(n, p) \) is constructed by starting with a set of \( n \) vertices.
elements.sent-2411-153: Each possible pair of vertices is then connected by an edge with independent probability \( p \).
elements.sent-2411-154: The probability \( p \) is a fixed parameter of the model, and it determines the expected density of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-155: When \( p \) is close to 0, the graph is sparse, while when \( p \) is close to 1, the graph is dense.
elements.sent-2411-156: G(n, m) model: In this version, a graph \( G(n, m) \) is formed by choosing exactly \( m \) edges uniformly at random from the set of all possible \( \binom{n {2 \) edges between \( n \) vertices.}}
elements.sent-2411-157: This model is often used when the exact number of edges is known, rather than the probability of edge formation.
elements.sent-2411-158: However, for $m\approx np$, the two models are asymptotically equivalent \cite{JLR00 .}
elements.sent-2411-159: Note that the degree of each vertex is distributed as a $\mathrm{Bin (n-1,p)$ random variable.}
elements.sent-2411-160: Thus for sparse regime $p = \lambda/n$, the asymptotic degree of each vertex is $\mathrm{Poisson (\lambda)$, with fixed average degree~$\lambda$.}
elements.sent-2411-161: While the Erd\H{o s-R\'{e nyi model is valuable for its simplicity, it assumes that all vertices have approximately the same degree in distribution (number of connections), and very few vertices of high degree (i.e.\ no power-law), which is not representative of many real-world networks.}}
elements.sent-2411-162: The configuration model addresses this limitation by allowing for more flexibility in the degree distribution of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-163: Consider a non-increasing sequence of degrees $\boldsymbol{d = ( d_i )_{i \in [n]}$ such that $\ell_n = \sum_{i \in [n] d_i$ is even.}}
elements.sent-2411-164: The configuration model on $n$ vertices having degree sequence $\boldsymbol{d}$ is constructed as follows \cite{B80,BC78 :.}
elements.sent-2411-165: Equip vertex $j$ with $d_{j}$ stubs, or half-edges.
elements.sent-2411-166: Two half-edges create an edge once they are paired.
elements.sent-2411-167: Therefore, initially we have $\ell_n=\sum_{i \in [n] d_i$ half-edges.}
elements.sent-2411-168: Pick any one half-edge and pair it with a uniformly chosen half-edge from the remaining unpaired half-edges and keep repeating the above procedure until all the unpaired half-edges are exhausted.
elements.sent-2411-169: Let $\rm{CM}$ denote the graph constructed by the above procedure.
elements.sent-2411-170: Note that $\rm{CM}$ may contain self-loops or multiple edges.
elements.sent-2411-171: It was shown in \cite{AHH19,JL18 that, under very general assumptions, the asymptotic probability of the graph being simple is positive.}
elements.sent-2411-172: The configuration model is particularly useful for studying networks with heterogeneous degree distributions, such as power-law distributions, which are common in many real-life networks, including social, biological, and technological systems.
elements.sent-2411-173: Configuration model is used to study the impact of degree heterogeneity on various network properties, such as connectivity, resilience, and the spread of information or diseases in \cite{T07,BST09,BST10 .}
elements.sent-2411-174: Inhomogeneous random graphs generalize the idea of random graphs by allowing the probability of edge formation to vary across different pairs of vertices.
elements.sent-2411-175: This model is more flexible and can capture the varying strengths of interactions or affinities between vertices that are often observed in real-life networks.
elements.sent-2411-176: A detailed analysis of the properties of this random graph model has been provided in \cite{BJR07 under a very general setup.}
elements.sent-2411-177: In an inhomogeneous random graph, each vertex \( i \) is assigned a weight \( w_i \), and the probability \( p_{ij \) that an edge exists between vertices \( i \) and \( j \) depends on their weights.}
elements.sent-2411-178: A common form for this probability is given by \begin{equation}p_{ij} = f(w_i, w_j)~,\end{equation} where \( f \) is a function that typically increases with \( w_i \) and \( w_j \).
elements.sent-2411-179: For example, in some models, \( p_{ij \) might be proportional to the product of the weights, \( p_{ij \propto w_i w_j \), which can lead to graphs where vertices with higher weights (representing more important or active entities) are more likely to be connected.}}
elements.sent-2411-180: Depending on the choice of the function \( f \), there are several models in the literature namely Norros-Reittu model \cite{NR06 , Chung-Lu model \cite{CL02,CL03 , Generalised random graph \cite{BDM06 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Choosing \( f \) function to be a constant \( p\in (0,1) \), we obtain the Erd\H{o s-R\'{e nyi model back from inhomogeneous random graphs.}}
elements.sent-2411-182: There are several other models available in the literature to accommodate different features of real-life networks, e.g.\ the random geometric graph was introduced in \cite{G61 to capture the underlying geometry of the networks.}
elements.sent-2411-183: This network has been in use to study social networks in \cite{WPR06,NA73,LC18,WW90 as it can encode actual physical distance, such as two servers in adjacent cities, as well as a more abstract form of similarity, e.g., users with similar interests or hobbies.}
elements.sent-2411-184: The Barab\'asi-Albert model of \cite{ABrB,albert1999 is {one of the most popular random graph model for dynamic real-life networks due to the fact that, through a simple dynamic, its properties resemble real-world networks.}}
elements.sent-2411-185: This model has been generalised in many different ways, creating a wide variety of {\em preferential attachment models (PAMs).}
elements.sent-2411-186: By preferential attachment models we denote a class of random graphs with a common dynamics: At every time step, a new vertex appears in the graph, and it connects {to $m\geq1$ existing vertices, with probability proportional to a function of the degrees \FC{of the vertices .}}
elements.sent-2411-187: In other words, when vertex $n\in\N$ appears, all of its edges connects to vertex $i\leq n$ with probability \eqn{ \pr(n\rightsquigarrow i \mid \mathrm{PA _{n-1 ) \propto f(d_i(n-1)), where \FC{$\PA_{n}$ is the preferential attachment graph with $n$ vertices, $d_i(n-1)$ denotes the degree of vertex $i$ in the graph $\PA_{n-1}$, and $f$ is some \FC{preferential attachment function.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-188: We {thus in fact {consider a whole PA class of random graphs since every function $f$ defines a different model.}}
elements.sent-2411-189: The original Barab\'asi-Albert model is retrieved with $f(k) = k$.
elements.sent-2411-190: The literature often considers the so-called {\em affine PAM, where $f(k) = k+\delta$, for some constant $\delta>-m$.}
elements.sent-2411-191: The constant $\delta$ allows for flexibility in the graph structure.

elements.sent-2411-245: Stochastic processes on random graphs combine the randomness inherent in graph structures with dynamic processes that live on these graphs.
elements.sent-2411-246: A stochastic process is a collection of random variables that evolves over time or space, and when these processes are applied to random graphs, they model a variety of phenomena in complex networks, such as random walks, the spread of information, diseases, or cascading failures.
elements.sent-2411-247: Random graphs, provide a flexible framework for studying how these processes behave in networks with different structural properties.
elements.sent-2411-248: The interplay between the topology of the graph and the stochastic dynamics can lead to rich and often surprising behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-249: For instance, the speed and pattern of disease spread in a population can be highly sensitive to the degree distribution of the underlying social network.
elements.sent-2411-250: In the next section, we study properties of percolation and Ising models on graphs.
elements.sent-2411-251: Percolation theory is a fundamental concept in the study of random processes living on networks and graphs.
elements.sent-2411-252: It examines the behaviour of connected clusters in a random graph as edges or vertices are added or removed randomly.
elements.sent-2411-253: Percolation is particularly useful in understanding the robustness and connectivity of networks, the spread of diseases, and various phase transitions in statistical physics.
elements.sent-2411-254: Given a graph $G$, bond (or site) percolation refers to the deletion of each edge (or vertex) independently with probability $1-p$.
elements.sent-2411-255: Throughout this discussion, we focus on bond percolation, hence we will simply refer to it as percolation.
elements.sent-2411-256: The resulting graph is denoted by $G(p)$.
elements.sent-2411-257: In the case of percolation on random graphs, the deletion of edges is independent of the structure of the underlying random graph.
elements.sent-2411-258: The percolation process is defined as the graph-valued stochastic process $(G(p))_{p \in [0,1]}$, which is coupled through the so-called Harris coupling.. More precisely:.
elements.sent-2411-259: Associate an independent uniform $[0,1]$ random variable $U_e$ to each edge $e$ of the graph $G$.
elements.sent-2411-260: The graph $G(p)$ is generated by retaining edge $e$ if and only if $U_e \leq p$.
elements.sent-2411-261: By keeping the uniform random variables fixed while varying $p$, we obtain a coupling between the graphs $(G(p))_{p \in [0,1]}$.
elements.sent-2411-262: Percolation has been extensively studied on infinite connected graphs, such as the hypercubic lattice.
elements.sent-2411-263: This is a very simple model that exhibits a phase transition.
elements.sent-2411-264: For an in-depth exploration of percolation theory, we refer the reader to \cite{JL18, FvdHdHH17, CHL09, Grimmet99, RvdHSF and the references therein.}
elements.sent-2411-265: Percolation is also employed to model vaccination strategies on a network, aiming to prevent the spread of an epidemic.
elements.sent-2411-266: A detailed account of these applications can be found in \cite{Newman-book, BBCS05, N02 .}
elements.sent-2411-267: Percolation on infinite trees offers a simpler setting to study phase transition phenomena, due to the absence of loops and the regular structure of trees.
elements.sent-2411-268: An infinite tree is a graph where any two nodes are connected by exactly one path, and the number of nodes is infinite.
elements.sent-2411-269: For an infinite tree, the percolation process begins by assigning each edge an independent probability \( p \) of being open.
elements.sent-2411-270: The key question is whether an infinite connected component of open edges exists.
elements.sent-2411-271: The critical probability \( p_c \) is defined as the infimum of the values of \( p \) for which an infinite connected component appears with non-zero probability.
elements.sent-2411-272: Let \( (\Tbold,o) \) be an infinite tree rooted at $o$, and \( (\Tbold(p),o) \) denote the percolated cluster of \( \Tbold(p) \) containing \(o\).
elements.sent-2411-273: Then, \(p_c\) is formally defined as \begin{equation}p_c = \inf\big\{ p\in[0,1]:~ \prob\big((\Tbold(p),o)~is infinite\big)>0 \big\}~.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-274: For $d$-regular infinite trees, where each vertex has the same degree \( d \), the critical probability \( p_c \) can be calculated explicitly as \begin{equation}p_c = \frac{1}{d-1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-275: When \( p < p_c \), all connected components are finite, and no infinite cluster exists, whereas for \( p > p_c \), an infinite cluster appears with positive probability.
elements.sent-2411-276: Percolation on branching processes is a useful technique for analysing the emergence of large connected components in random structures.
elements.sent-2411-277: It is important to note that, if the root is retained in site percolation, performing bond and site percolation on branching processes are equivalent operations.
elements.sent-2411-278: While percolating a branching process, we retain or delete individuals (or equivalently, edges in the associated tree) independently, with a given probability \( p \).
elements.sent-2411-279: The critical probability \( p_c \) in percolation on branching processes is the threshold above which an infinite cluster (corresponding to an infinitely large connected component) emerges.
elements.sent-2411-280: Below this threshold, all components remain finite.
elements.sent-2411-281: The analysis of percolation on branching processes is particularly elegant because the branching structure allows for precise calculations of critical probabilities and cluster sizes, and due to the fact that a percolated branching process remains a branching process.
elements.sent-2411-282: For a simple Galton-Watson branching process with a mean offspring number \( \mu \), the critical percolation threshold \( p_c \) is determined by the condition that the expected number of surviving offspring per individual equals \(1\), i.e., \( p_c \mu = 1 \).. Thus, \begin{equation}p_c = \frac{1}{\mu}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-283: Heuristically, the inverse of the growth rate is the critical percolation threshold, for an exponentially growing branching process.
elements.sent-2411-284: In contrast to infinite trees, finite random graphs introduce additional complexity due to their finite size and the presence of loops.
elements.sent-2411-285: Percolation on finite random graphs involves studying the formation of large connected components as edges are randomly included with probability \( p \).
elements.sent-2411-286: In this setting, the concept of a phase transition is still relevant but manifests differently due to the finite size of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-287: As \( p \) increases, a critical threshold \( p_c \) is reached, beyond which a giant connected component emerges, containing a substantial fraction of the total number of vertices.
elements.sent-2411-288: Below \( p_c \), most components remain small and fragmented.
elements.sent-2411-289: Now, we define the critical percolation threshold for a sequence of graph \(\{G_n\}_{n\geq 1}\).
elements.sent-2411-290: For any \(p\in[0,1]\), let \(\Ccal_1^{(n) (p)\) denote the largest connected component of the \(p\)-percolated graph \(G_n\).}
elements.sent-2411-291: Then the critical percolation threshold \(p_c\) for the graph sequence \(\{G_n\}_{n\geq 1}\) is defined as \begin{equation}p_c=\inf\Big\{ p\in[0,1]:~\chRounak{\liminf\limits_{n\to\infty}\prob\Big(\frac{|\Ccal_1^{(n)}(p)|}{n}>\vep\Big)=1}~for some \vep>0 \Big\}~.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-292: The behavior of percolation on finite random graphs is often studied in the context of specific random graph models, such as the Erd\H{o s-R\'{e nyi models, or configuration models.}}
elements.sent-2411-293: It's noteworthy that Erd\H{o s-R\'{e nyi random graphs are essentially percolated complete graphs, and hence a percolated Erd\H{o s-R\'{e nyi graph is again an Erd\H{o s-R\'{e nyi graph.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-294: In the Erd\H{o s-R\'{e nyi graph \( G(n, p) \), where \( n \) is the number of vertices and each edge is included with probability \( p \), \cite{ER59,G59 proved that the critical probability \( p_c \) is given by \begin{equation}p_c = \frac{1}{n-1}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-295: For \( p > p_c(1+\vep) \) and any $\vep>0$, a giant component emerges, typically containing \( O(n) \) vertices.
elements.sent-2411-296: For \( p < p_c \), the graph consists mostly of small components.
elements.sent-2411-298: The percolation phase transition on random regular graphs and configuration models were studied in \cite{F07,J09 when the empirical degree distribution has a finite second moment in the large network limit, as $n\to\infty$.}
elements.sent-2411-299: See also \cite{FJP22,JP18 for some recent results on more general degree sequences.}
elements.sent-2411-300: In this thesis, we study our recent work on computing the critical percolation threshold for preferential attachment models explicitly.
elements.sent-2411-301: The Ising model originated in the early \(20\)-th century as a simple yet powerful framework to study phase transitions and collective behaviour in magnetic systems.
elements.sent-2411-302: It was first introduced by Wilhelm Lenz in \cite{L1920 and further developed by his student, Ernst Ising, in \cite{I1925 .}}
elements.sent-2411-303: The model was initially conceived to explain ferromagnetism, a phenomenon where atomic spins align to create a permanent magnet.
elements.sent-2411-304: In its most basic form, the Ising model considers a graph structure where each site (or node) hosts a spin that can take one of two values, typically represented as \(+1\) or \(-1\).
elements.sent-2411-305: These spins interact with their nearest neighbours, favouring alignment to minimise the system's energy.
elements.sent-2411-306: The interplay between thermal fluctuations, that tend to randomise spin orientation, and the interactions, which seek to align spins, gives rise to interesting collective phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-307: In the Ising model, we can observe a phase transition in terms of the abrupt change in the system's macroscopic properties as the temperature crosses a critical threshold, often known as the Curie temperature in physics literature.
elements.sent-2411-308: At high temperatures, thermal fluctuations dominate, causing the spins to orient randomly, resulting in a disordered phase with no net magnetisation.
elements.sent-2411-309: However, as the temperature decreases and falls below a critical value (known as the critical temperature), the system undergoes a phase transition to an ordered phase.
elements.sent-2411-310: In the low-temperature phase, the spins tend to align, leading to a spontaneous magnetisation where the system exhibits a net magnetic moment.
elements.sent-2411-311: This phase transition is a hallmark of the Ising model, illustrating how microscopic interactions can lead to emergent macroscopic behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-312: Let $(G_n)_{n\geq 1}$ be a sequence of (possibly random) graphs, where $G_n=(V_n,E_n)$ has vertex set $V_n=\{1,2,\ldots,n\}=[n]$, and some (possibly random) set of edges $E_n$.
elements.sent-2411-313: Each vertex in $G_n$ is assigned an Ising spin value in $\{-1,+1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-314: A spin configuration of $G_n$ is denoted by $\sigma=(\sigma_1,\ldots,\sigma_n)\in\{-1,+1\}^n$.
elements.sent-2411-315: For any $\beta\geq 0$ and $B_v\in\R$ for all $v\in V_n$, the Ising model on $G_n$ is given by the Boltzmann distribution \begin{equation}\label{eq:def:boltzmann:seq} \mu_n(\sigma)=\frac{1}{Z_n(\beta,B)}\exp\left\{ \beta\sum\limits_{\{u,v\}\in E_n}\sigma_u\sigma_v +\sum\limits_{v\in V_n}B_v\sigma_v \right\},\end{equation} where $Z_n({\beta,B )$ is the normalising constant, also known as the partition function, given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:def:partition-function} Z_n({\beta,B})=\sum\limits_{\sigma\in\{-1,+1\}^n}\exp\left\{ \beta\sum\limits_{\{u,v\}\in E_n}\sigma_u\sigma_v +\sum\limits_{v\in V_n}B_v\sigma_v \right\}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-316: For any function $f:\{-1,+1\}^n\to \R,$ define $\langle f(\sigma) \rangle_{\mu_n}$ as \begin{equation}\label{eq:def:quenched-expectation} \langle f(\sigma) \rangle_{\mu_n} =\sum\limits_{\sigma\in\{-1,+1\}^n} f(\sigma)\mu_n(\sigma).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-317: Lastly, we define the thermodynamic quantity of main interest, pressure per particle, as \begin{equation}\label{eq:def:pressure-per-particle:initial} \psi_n(\beta,B)=\frac{1}{n}\log Z_n(\beta,B).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-318: Ernst Ising proved in \cite{I1925 that the Ising model on \(\mathbb{Z}\) does not exhibit a phase transition, meaning the critical temperature in this case is \(\infty\).}
elements.sent-2411-319: For the Ising model on \(\mathbb{Z ^2\), Onsager obtained the explicit thermodynamic limits of several quantities in \cite{O44 , which were later extensively generalised by Montroll, Potts, and Ward in \cite{MPW63 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-320: From two dimensions onwards, the Ising model is known to exhibit a phase transition.
elements.sent-2411-321: The behaviour of the Ising model in higher dimensions is explored in detail in \cite{BKLS89 .}
elements.paragraph-2411-019: Ising model on the random graphs
elements.sent-2411-322: Alongside the Ising model on lattice structures, it has also been studied on complete graphs and other types of graphs, as discussed in \cite{KPW13, EE88, CG16 .}
elements.sent-2411-323: The Ising model on random graphs introduces a second level of randomness due to the inherent variability of the graph itself.
elements.sent-2411-324: This leads to two distinct settings: quenched and annealed.
elements.sent-2411-325: In the annealed setting, the Ising model is examined after averaging out the randomness of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-326: In contrast, the quenched setting involves studying the Ising model conditioned on a specific realisation of the random graph.
elements.sent-2411-327: For a detailed analysis of Ising models on random graphs, see \cite{B06, CG13, E06 .}
elements.sent-2411-328: Beyond its original application to ferromagnetism, the Ising model has become relevant in a wide range of disciplines, including biology, sociology, and computer science.
elements.sent-2411-329: It is employed to model binary systems with local interactions, such as gene regulation networks, social dynamics, and optimisation problems.
elements.sent-2411-330: For applications in biological sciences, see \cite{KTS20,WB16 ; for applications in social sciences, refer to \cite{FS19 ; and for applications in computer science and optimisation, see \cite{CZSHT18,BKNKOS22 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-331: The Ising model's mathematical elegance and capacity to model critical behaviour ensure its status as a cornerstone of theoretical physics and applied mathematics.
elements.sent-2411-332: In the study of sparse random graphs, various tools and techniques have been developed to analyse their complex structures and dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-333: Due to the sparse edge density characteristic of these graphs, the neighbourhood of a uniformly chosen vertex often resembles a branching process, particularly when considering small, fixed neighbourhoods.
elements.sent-2411-334: This resemblance was rigorously formalised through the concept of local convergence, introduced by Benjamini and Schramm in \cite{Benjamini_LL .}
elements.sent-2411-335: Local convergence has since become a fundamental method for studying the limiting behaviour of local structures in random graphs, such as degree distribution and clustering.
elements.sent-2411-336: Beyond local properties, some global characteristics such as the critical percolation threshold or the behaviour of the Ising model can also be derived using local limits, albeit under specific model constraints.
elements.sent-2411-337: These global properties provide crucial insights into the overall behaviour of the graph, bridging the gap between local structure and global dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-338: The preferential attachment model presents additional challenges due to its dynamic nature, where edges are added sequentially and depend on the existing network structure.
elements.sent-2411-339: This sequential dependence introduces complexities that require specialized techniques to handle.
elements.sent-2411-340: One common approach is the collapsing method, which transforms preferential attachment trees into graphs, simplifying the analysis.
elements.sent-2411-341: Another useful technique is the P\'{o lya urn representation from \cite{BBCS05 , which is employed to achieve conditional independence between edge-connection events, facilitating the study of these models.}}
elements.sent-2411-342: Additionally, the continuous-time branching process representation is often utilised to analyse preferential attachment models, as the literature on branching processes is more mature and offers a wealth of applicable results.
elements.sent-2411-343: In this section, we will explore these tools and techniques in detail, discussing how they are applied to the study of sparse random graphs and, more specifically, preferential attachment models.
elements.sent-2411-344: These methods are essential for gaining a deeper understanding of the structure and behaviour of these complex networks.
elements.sent-2411-345: Branching processes are a class of random models that describe the evolution of populations where individuals reproduce independently according to some probability distribution.
elements.sent-2411-346: Branching processes are classified into two major settings: the discrete-time branching process and the continuous-time branching process.
elements.sent-2411-347: The simplest discrete-time branching process is the Galton-Watson process, which models a population where each individual in one generation produces a random number of offspring in the next generation, independently of other individuals.
elements.sent-2411-348: The process starts with a single ancestor, and the population evolves through generations.
elements.sent-2411-349: In general, when a branching process is infinite, it tends to grow exponentially (and sometimes even doubly exponentially).
elements.sent-2411-350: For branching processes with exponential growth, the exponential growth parameter is denoted by \( \lambda \).
elements.sent-2411-351: In cases where the mean number of offspring \( \mu > 1 \), the population typically grows exponentially, and \( \lambda \) represents the rate of this growth.
elements.sent-2411-352: Heuristically, if \( Z_n \) denotes the size of the \( n \)-th generation, the exponential growth parameter satisfies \begin{equation}\lim_{n \to \infty} \E[Z_n]^{1/n} = \lambda~,\end{equation} indicating that \( \E[Z_n] \) grows approximately as \( \lambda^{ n \) for large \( n \).}
elements.sent-2411-353: The exponential growth parameter plays a crucial role in determining the long-term behaviour of the process, including whether the population will grow indefinitely or face extinction.
elements.sent-2411-354: While the basic Galton-Watson process assumes a single type of individual, many real-world populations are more complex, involving individuals of different types, each with its own reproduction behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-355: This leads to multitype branching processes, where the population consists of individuals of several types, and the offspring distribution depends on the type of the parent.
elements.sent-2411-356: In a multitype branching process with countably many types, the evolution of the population can be described by a vector \( \mathbf{Z _n \) representing the number of individuals of each type in the \( n \)-th generation.}
elements.sent-2411-357: The process is governed by a matrix of reproduction probabilities, where each entry specifies the expected number of offspring of a particular type produced by an individual of another type.
elements.sent-2411-358: The exponential growth parameter generalises to multitype branching processes with countably many types, where it is defined as the largest eigenvalue of the mean offspring matrix.
elements.sent-2411-359: As discussed in \cite{AN72,H63 , this parameter again determines the asymptotic growth rate of the population, providing insights into the long-term dynamics of multitype populations.}
elements.sent-2411-360: A further extension of the multitype branching process with countably many types is the multitype branching process with a continuous type space.
elements.sent-2411-361: The mean offspring matrix ensemble for this extension is known as the mean offspring operator.
elements.sent-2411-362: Under certain regularity conditions, \cite{AN72,H63,A2000,LPP95 proved that the spectral radius of the mean offspring operator determines the exponential growth of this branching process.}
elements.sent-2411-363: A continuous-time branching process (CTBP) is a stochastic model used to describe the evolution of populations where individuals reproduce and die according to random processes that occur continuously over time.
elements.sent-2411-364: Unlike discrete-time branching processes, where events happen at fixed intervals, in CTBPs, the time between events (such as births and deaths) is typically modelled by exponential distributions, allowing for greater flexibility in capturing real-world dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-365: In a CTBP, each individual lives for a random amount of time before either reproducing or dying, and the offspring produced by an individual can also reproduce or die, creating a branching structure over time.
elements.sent-2411-366: The process starts with an initial population, and the size and composition of the population evolve as individuals are added or removed.
elements.sent-2411-367: The key parameters of a CTBP include the birth rate, death rate, and the distribution of the number of offspring produced.
elements.sent-2411-368: One of the fundamental properties of CTBPs is the Malthusian parameter, which determines the long-term growth rate of the population.
elements.sent-2411-369: This parameter is analogous to the exponential growth parameter in discrete-time branching processes.
elements.sent-2411-370: If this parameter is positive, the population tends to grow exponentially over time; if it is negative, the population is likely to die out.
elements.sent-2411-371: Significant contributions in this area have been made by Jagers and Nerman in \cite{JN84 and Nerman in \cite{N81 .}}
elements.sent-2411-372: CTBPs are widely used in various fields, including biology, epidemiology, and network theory, to model phenomena where the timing of events is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-373: Since preferential attachment trees grow over time, \cite{AGS08 provided a continuous-time branching process ensemble for preferential attachment trees.}
elements.sent-2411-374: This CTBP representation of preferential attachment trees has been utilised in analysing degree distributions and PageRank asymptotics in preferential attachment trees in \cite{BO22, BDO23, GHL20, RTV07 .}
elements.sent-2411-375: Branching processes and their multitype extensions are useful tools in various fields.
elements.sent-2411-376: In biology, they model the spread of genes or species; in epidemiology, they describe the transmission of infectious diseases; and in network theory, they help to understand the growth of complex networks.
elements.sent-2411-377: For applications of branching processes in biological sciences, see for example \cite{KA02,CMMP06 .}
elements.sent-2411-378: The ability to capture both the probabilistic nature and the heterogeneity of populations makes branching processes an essential component of modern applied mathematics.
elements.sent-2411-379: Motivated by the growing importance of dynamic networks in real-world applications, the mathematics community has devoted significant attention to the study of graph limits over the past five years.
elements.sent-2411-380: Much of this work has focused on dense graphs, particularly through the lens of graph homomorphisms.
elements.sent-2411-381: A notable series of papers \cite{BCLSV06, BCLSV08, BCLSV12, LS06 has developed a comprehensive framework for graph limits defined via graph homomorphisms, demonstrating their equivalence to limits defined through other approaches \cite{BCLSV08, BCLSV12 .}}
elements.sent-2411-382: While the majority of these results pertain to dense graphs, the seminal paper \cite{BCLSV06 also introduces a notion of graph limits for sparse graphs with bounded degrees, again using graph homomorphisms.}
elements.sent-2411-383: Furthering this line of inquiry, Borgs et al. \cite{BCKL13 , employing expansion methods from mathematical physics, established general results on graph limits for sparse graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-384: Another significant contribution is found in \cite{BR11 , which addresses the limits of graphs that are neither dense nor sparse in the conventional sense, focusing on graphs whose average degrees tend to infinity.}
elements.sent-2411-385: Local convergence techniques have become a key methodology to study sparse random graphs.
elements.sent-2411-386: These important properties include the thermodynamic quantities like pressure per particle, magnetization, internal energy etc. However, convergence of many random graph properties does not directly follow from local convergence.
elements.sent-2411-387: A notable, and important, such random graph property is the size and uniqueness of the giant component.
elements.sent-2411-388: In \cite[Conjecture~1.2]{BNP11 , Schramm conjectured that the critical percolation threshold exhibits locality for a sequence of vertex-transitive infinite graphs converging locally to a limiting graph.}
elements.sent-2411-389: Various attempts have been made to prove this conjecture for different subclasses of infinite graphs \cite{BNP11,S21,ABS22,KLS20,vdH23 .}
elements.sent-2411-390: In particular, van der Hofstad in \cite{vdH23 provided a simple necessary and sufficient condition for the conjecture to hold, while \cite{BNP11,S21,KLS20 proved it for bounded-degree expanders.}}
elements.sent-2411-391: Recently, Easo and Hutchcroft proved Schramm's conjecture in \cite{EH23 .}
elements.sent-2411-392: Furthermore, \cite{ABS22 established the conjecture for large-set expander graphs with bounded average degree and extended the result to include directed percolation as well.}
elements.sent-2411-393: This result extends Schramm's conjecture beyond the scope of vertex-transitive graphs.
elements.sent-2411-394: All these results provide an estimate of the asymptotic size of the largest and second largest connected components in the percolated graph sequence.
elements.sent-2411-395: Since this asymptotic result is not a direct consequence of local convergence, the giant is said to be almost local.
elements.paragraph-2411-023: Locality of Ising pressure per particle
elements.sent-2411-396: Unlike the critical percolation threshold or the largest connected component, Ising thermodynamic quantities are local properties of random graphs in quenched settings.
elements.sent-2411-397: Dembo and Montanari proved in \cite{DM10 that these properties are local for random graphs with finite variance degree sequences in quenched settings.}
elements.sent-2411-398: Later, Dommers et al. extended these results in \cite{DGvdH10 to cases with infinite variance degree sequences.}
elements.sent-2411-399: Finally, Dembo, Montanari, and Sun generalised this result to all random graphs under a uniform integrability condition on the degree sequence in \cite{DMS13 .}
elements.sent-2411-400: They considered factor models, including Ising and Potts models as special cases, where this locality result holds true.
elements.sent-2411-401: The annealed setting is often easier to handle and is therefore used as an approximation to the quenched setting.
elements.sent-2411-402: Examples include spin glasses \cite{CG13 , such as the Sherrington-Kirkpatrick model, where the annealed partition function becomes trivial due to the independence of the couplings, and random polymers, where the comparison between quenched and annealed settings is used to distinguish between relevant and irrelevant disorder \cite{CdHP12 .}}
elements.sent-2411-403: In the physics literature, it is generally believed that the annealed Ising model on a random graph is equivalent to an inhomogeneous mean-field model where the coupling between spins is proportional to their degrees; see Bianconi \cite{B02 and Dorogovtsev et al. \cite{dorogovtsev2008 .}}
elements.sent-2411-404: The annealed setting of the Ising model was first studied on random graphs in \cite{DGGvdHP16 .}
elements.sent-2411-405: Leveraging the locally tree-like property, Can examined the annealed Ising model on random regular graphs in \cite{C17 , and later Can et al. \cite{CGGvdH22 studied the model on configuration models in both random and fixed degree settings.}}
elements.sent-2411-406: For fixed degree settings, they found the physics folklore to hold true, whereas for random degree settings of the configuration model, the conjecture did not hold.
elements.subsection-2411-007: Tree-like structure of preferential attachment models
elements.sent-2411-407: Sparse Erd\H{o s–R\'{e nyi graphs, where the average degree remains constant as the number of vertices grows, the local limit is a Poisson branching process.}}
elements.sent-2411-408: This means that, locally, the graph around any randomly chosen vertex resembles a tree where the number of offspring (neighbours) of each node follows a Poisson distribution.
elements.sent-2411-409: Similarly, other classes of sparse random graphs, such as configuration models and generalised random graphs, also admit local limits that are branching processes.
elements.sent-2411-410: In these cases, the degree distribution of the vertices influences the branching process, leading to different types of local tree-like structures that capture the graph's local behaviour as the graph size tends to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-411: These results consistently identify the local limit as a multi-type branching process with a continuous type space, a topic that we explore in detail in Chapter~\ref{chap:LWC .}
elements.sent-2411-412: Additionally, \cite{DM13 identified a killed branching random walk as the local limit of the sub-linear Bernoulli preferential attachment model, as defined in \cite{vdH2 .}}
elements.sent-2411-413: Collectively, these findings suggest that, locally, almost all preferential attachment models have tree-like structures.
elements.sent-2411-414: Continuous time branching processes (CTBPs) are known to embed preferential attachment trees, which are typically defined as a discrete sequence of random graphs, into continuous time \cite{A07, AGS08, B07, RTV07 .}
elements.sent-2411-415: The heuristic idea is to construct a CTBP whose tree in continuous time coincides with a preferential attachment tree along the sequence of birth times.
elements.sent-2411-416: This approach offers the advantage of leveraging the extensive theory of CTBPs to investigate the properties of preferential attachment trees.
elements.sent-2411-417: Contrary to other random graph models, preferential attachment models are dynamic graphs with a power-law degree distribution.
elements.sent-2411-418: Furthermore, the edge-connection events in preferential attachment models are highly dependent.
elements.sent-2411-419: While these properties make the model more representative of real-life networks, they also complicate the analysis of its local and global properties.
elements.sent-2411-420: To tackle problems related to preferential attachment models, two key tools are essential: the P\'{o lya urn representation and the collapsing operator.}
elements.sent-2411-421: This is a classic stochastic process that models the evolution of a system where the likelihood of certain outcomes increases as those outcomes occur more frequently.
elements.sent-2411-422: The process begins with an urn containing balls of different colours.
elements.sent-2411-423: At each step, a ball is drawn at random, its colour is noted, and then it is returned to the urn along with additional balls of the same colour.
elements.sent-2411-424: This reinforcement mechanism makes it more likely to draw balls of the same colour in future steps, leading to a form of positive feedback.
elements.sent-2411-425: Observe that, despite being correlated, the ball drawing events exchangeable.
elements.sent-2411-426: We can then use de Finnetti's lemma to show that this system is equivalent to the following simpler to analyse stochastic process:.
elements.sent-2411-427: For every colour \(i\), choose a parameter (which we call strength or attractiveness) \( p_i \), and at each step, independently of our decision in previous steps, put the new ball in urn \(i\) with probability \(p_i\).
elements.sent-2411-428: With suitably chosen prior distribution for \(p_i\), this procedure is equivalent to the P\'{o lya urn process.}
elements.sent-2411-429: The P\'{o lya urn process is a fundamental tool in probability theory, with applications ranging from the study of network growth models to understanding preferential attachment mechanisms.}
elements.sent-2411-430: It captures the essence of "rich-get-richer" dynamics, where initial advantages can amplify over time, making it particularly useful in modelling systems with self-reinforcing behaviours.
elements.sent-2411-431: The edge-connection process in preferential attachment models is closely related to the P\'{o lya urn scheme in the following sense: every new connection that a vertex gains can be represented by a new ball added in the urn corresponding to that vertex, and every new vertex creates a new colour in the urn.}
elements.sent-2411-432: Berger et al. used this idea to obtain the P\'{o lya urn representation of the sequential models of the preferential attachment models in \cite{BBCS05 , where conditionally on a sequence of $\Beta$ random variables, the edge-connection events are independent.}}
elements.sent-2411-433: Collapsing is a valuable technique for studying affine preferential attachment models.
elements.sent-2411-434: This facilitates the transition from tree-based versions of these models to their non-tree counterparts.

elements.sent-2411-454: Part I focuses on the local limit of generalised preferential attachment models and consists of three chapters.
elements.sent-2411-455: In Chapter~\ref{chap:LWC , we introduce the concept of local limit in detail and describe the random P\'{o lya point tree.}}
elements.sent-2411-456: We also demonstrate that the P\'{o lya point tree described in \cite{BergerBorgs is a special case of our more general random P\'{o lya point tree.}}}
elements.sent-2411-457: Chapter~\ref{chap:PU_representation presents the P\'{o lya urn representation of sequential preferential attachment models and proves that this representation is distributionally equivalent to the model.}}
elements.sent-2411-458: Chapter~\ref{chap:convergence concludes Part I by proving the local convergence of generalised preferential attachment models to the random P\'{o lya point tree.}}
elements.sent-2411-459: As a consequence of this local limit, we compute the degree distribution of a uniformly chosen vertex and its neighbours.
elements.sent-2411-460: Part II investigates stochastic processes on preferential attachment models, with a particular emphasis on percolation and the Ising model.
elements.sent-2411-461: Chapter~\ref{chap:percolation_threshold_PPT begins by computing the explicit critical percolation threshold for P\'{o lya point trees and proves that the inverse of the spectral radius of the mean offspring operator of P\'{o lya point tree is the critical percolation threshold in the positive $\delta$ regime.}}}
elements.sent-2411-462: During this proof, we establish the continuity of the survival probability of the P\'{o lya point tree with respect to the percolation probability.}
elements.sent-2411-463: In Chapter~\ref{chap:local-giant , we extend this result to preferential attachment models, showing that they share the same critical percolation threshold as the P\'{o lya point tree, leveraging their properties as large-set expanders with bounded average degree and the continuity of the survival probability of their local limit.}}
elements.sent-2411-464: Chapter~\ref{chap:subcritical studies the size of the largest connected component in sub-critical percolated preferential attachment models, demonstrating that it is significantly larger than the maximum degree in the graph.}
elements.sent-2411-465: This chapter also includes simulation results to support our lower bound argument.
elements.sent-2411-466: Finally, Chapter~\ref{chap:ising examines the quenched Ising model on preferential attachment models, where we derive explicit thermodynamic limits for pressure per particle, magnetisation per vertex, and internal energy.}

elements.sent-2411-472: Local convergence of rooted graphs was introduced by Benjamini and Schramm in \cite{Benjamini_LL and by Aldous and Steele in \cite{Aldous2004 .}}
elements.sent-2411-473: A large class of random graphs are referred to as locally tree-like graphs, meaning that the neighbourhood of the majority of vertices is structured like a tree (up to a certain distance).
elements.sent-2411-474: This concept can be formalised using the notion of local convergence (i.e., the Benjamini-Schramm limit), introduced in \cite{Aldous2004,Benjamini_LL .}
elements.sent-2411-475: Local convergence has proven to be an extremely versatile tool for understanding the geometry of graphs.
elements.sent-2411-476: For example, the asymptotic number of spanning trees, the partition function of the Ising model, and the spectral distribution of the adjacency matrix of the graph can all be computed in terms of the local limit.
elements.sent-2411-477: The basic idea is to explore the neighbourhood of a uniformly chosen vertex of the graph up to a finite distance to understand its distributional and geometric properties.
elements.sent-2411-478: Marked local convergence of random graphs is relatively new to the literature.
elements.sent-2411-479: For an overview of the theory and applications of this concept, we refer to, e.g., \cite[{Chapter 2 ]{vdH2 .}}

elements.sent-2411-482: Lastly, in Section~\ref{chap:LWC:sec:relation-literature , we establish the connection between the P'{o lya point tree defined in Section~\ref{chap:LWC:sec:RPPT and the one defined in \cite{BergerBorgs .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-749: Again, for the joint density calculation, we use the P\'olya urn description of {our models.}
elements.sent-2411-750: Since the edge-connection {events are conditionally independent {by the P\'olya urn representations , the two neighbourhoods are {\em conditionally independent when they are disjoint.}}}
elements.sent-2411-751: Therefore the joint density {factorizes and we obtain the required result.}
elements.sent-2411-752: Though our main steps for proving the theorem {are the same as {those in \cite{BergerBorgs , our proof techniques differ {significantly , for example, we avoid the induction argument in the neighbourhood size.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-753: We use a coupling of the preferential attachment model with the P\'olya urn graphs through an explicit density computation.
elements.sent-2411-754: To summarize, we have two crucial steps in proving the main theorem:.
elements.sent-2411-755: Equivalence: There exist P\'olya urn representations for models (A), (B), and (D), which we have already proved in Chapter~\ref{chap:PU_representation .}
elements.sent-2411-756: Convergence: The joint density of the ages of the vertices in the $r$-neighbourhood of the P\'olya urn graphs converges to that of the RPPT, and this is the main content of this chapter.
elements.sent-2411-757: For model (E), the edges are connected independently and the degrees of the older vertices are updated only after the new vertex is included in the graph with {\em all its out-edges.}
elements.sent-2411-758: This edge-connection procedure is different from other models and we do not have a P\'olya urn representation for this graph.
elements.sent-2411-759: We prove the convergence of models with a P\'olya urn representation.
elements.sent-2411-760: We do not, however, prove local convergence for models (E) and (F) here.
elements.sent-2411-761: A heuristic approach would be to couple models (D) and (E) such that the probabilities of observing the $r$-neighbourhoods of a uniformly chosen vertex in the graphs are asymptotically equal.
elements.sent-2411-762: Since we have proven local convergence for model (D), the convergence for model (E) will follow immediately.
elements.sent-2411-763: Note, however, that this does not imply the convergence of the joint density of the ages of vertices in the $r$-neighbourhood for model (E).
elements.sent-2411-764: Model (F) can be addressed similarly to model (E).
elements.sent-2411-765: For analysing the convergence to the local limit, we need some analytical results for both $\CPU$ and $\RPPT$.
elements.sent-2411-766: Some of the proofs here follow those in \cite{BergerBorgs , while others include significant novel ideas.}
elements.sent-2411-767: We explain how these results can be reproduced for $\CPU^{\sss\rm{(NSL) }$ or $\PU$ at the end of this section.}
elements.sent-2411-768: This section is organised as follows.
elements.sent-2411-769: In Section \ref{subsec-prel-exp , we provide auxiliary results on the expected values of random variables.}
elements.sent-2411-770: In Section \ref{subsec-pos-conc-Beta-gamma-coupling , we use these results to analyse the asymptotics of the positions $\mathcal{S _k^{\sss(n)}$ and for an effective coupling of Beta and Gamma variables.}}
elements.sent-2411-771: Finally, in Section \ref{sec-attachment-RPPT , we use these results to study the asymptotics of the attachment probabilities, and we prove some regularity properties of the RPPT.}

elements.sent-2411-1123: In this chapter, we prove that the critical percolation threshold for the P\'olya point tree is the inverse of the spectral radius of the mean offspring operator.

elements.sent-2411-1127: Bond percolation is a fundamental and elementary process in statistical physics and network science that investigates the connectivity of a network under random attack.
elements.sent-2411-1128: In bond percolation, each edge of a given graph is independently retained with probability $\pi$ and removed with probability $1-\pi$.

elements.sent-2411-1134: Percolation on branching processes has been extensively studied, and we refer the reader to \cite{JN84,AN72,H63 and the references therein.}

elements.sent-2411-1137: Under certain conditions, it has been shown in \cite{AN72 that the spectral radius of the mean offspring operator of the branching process is its critical percolation threshold.}

elements.sent-2411-1462: In this chapter, we study the percolation phase transition on preferential attachment models, where vertices enter with $m$ edges and attach proportionally to their degree plus $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-1463: We identify the critical percolation threshold as \begin{equation}\pi_c = \frac{\delta}{2\left(m(m+\delta) + \sqrt{m(m-1)(m+\delta)(m+1+\delta)}\right)}\end{equation} for $\delta > 0$, and $\pi_c = 0$ for non-positive values of $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-1464: Therefore, the giant component is robust for $\delta \in (-m, 0]$, but not for $\delta > 0$.
elements.sent-2411-1465: First, we show that preferential attachment graphs are large-set expanders, enabling us to verify the conditions outlined in \cite{ABS22 .}
elements.sent-2411-1466: Under these conditions, the proportion of vertices in the largest connected component of a graph sequence converges to the survival probability of percolation on the local limit.
elements.sent-2411-1467: In particular, the critical percolation threshold for both the graph and its local limit are identical.
elements.sent-2411-1468: In Chapter~\ref{chap:percolation_threshold_PPT , we identified $\pi_c$ as the critical percolation threshold of the P\'olya point tree, which is the local limit of preferential attachment models, proving that $\pi_c$ is indeed the critical percolation threshold for a sequence of preferential attachment models.. \end{minipage \end{center.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1469: Random graph models typically undergo a phase transition in their connectivity structure depending on the percolation probability $\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-1470: Specifically, there exists a critical percolation threshold $\pi_c \in [0,1]$ such that for $\pi > \pi_c$, the proportion of vertices in the largest connected component is strictly positive, whereas for $\pi < \pi_c$, this proportion converges to zero.
elements.sent-2411-1471: In other words, \begin{equation}\label{def:critical-percolation} \frac{|\mathcal{C}_1^{(n)}(\pi)|}{n} \to c(\pi)~,\end{equation} where $c(\pi) > 0$ for $\pi > \pi_c$ and $c(\pi) = 0$ when $\pi < \pi_c$.
elements.sent-2411-1472: Here, $\mathcal{C _1^{(n) (\pi)$ denotes the largest connected component of the percolated graph of size $n$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1473: The value $\pi_c$ is known as the critical percolation threshold of the random graph.
elements.sent-2411-1474: Percolation on random graphs has a extensive and diverse literature, to which we refer the reader for more detailed information \cite{BJR07,ER60,vdH1,JL09,JPRR18,MR95 .}
elements.sent-2411-1475: Local convergence techniques have become valuable tools for studying the size and uniqueness of the giant component in these graphs.
elements.sent-2411-1476: However, it is important to note that these properties do not immediately follow from local convergence alone.
elements.sent-2411-1477: They hold true under certain well-connectedness conditions of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-1478: Although percolation on static random graphs has been extensively studied, percolation on preferential attachment models is relatively less explored.
elements.sent-2411-1479: In \cite{EMO21 , the authors studied the proportion of vertices in the largest connected component of the Bernoulli preferential attachment model defined in \cite{DM13 , where the attachment rule is a function of the number of incoming edges of a vertex.}}
elements.sent-2411-1480: In \cite{ABS22 , the preferential attachment model for the independent edge attachment rule and $\delta = 0$ was studied.}
elements.sent-2411-1481: It was shown that the model is a large-set expander with bounded average degree, and its critical percolation threshold is $0$.
elements.sent-2411-1482: Additionally, it was demonstrated that the phase transition is of infinite order in that case, as proved in \cite{EMO21 for the model in \cite{DM13 .}}
elements.sent-2411-1483: The fact that $\delta = 0$ plays a crucial role in this proof, which mainly relies on functional analysis.
elements.sent-2411-1484: In this chapter, we prove that the giant component of several other preferential attachment models with affine attachment functions and a general $\delta$ parameter is local, and the critical percolation thresholds for these models are all equal and match that of their local limit, the P\'{o lya point tree.}
elements.sent-2411-1485: Throughout this part, we shall only consider the deterministic version of the preferential attachment models.
elements.sent-2411-1486: For convenience, we describe the models here again.
elements.sent-2411-1487: We focus on sequential preferential attachment models, specifically models (a), (b), and (d) as defined in \cite{vdH1 , excluding their tree cases.}
elements.sent-2411-1489: In these models, every new vertex is introduced to the existing graph with $m$ edges incident to it.
elements.sent-2411-1490: The models are defined by their edge-connection probabilities.
