elements.sent-2411-1504: We define several notations that will be used throughout the chapter.
elements.sent-2411-1505: Let $G=(V(G),E(G))$ be an undirected multigraph with self-loops and multiple edges.
elements.sent-2411-1506: The degree of a vertex $u \in V(G)$ is denoted by $d_G(u)$.
elements.sent-2411-1507: Each self-loop contributes 2 to the degree of the vertex.
elements.sent-2411-1508: For any $S \subset V(G)$, the volume of the subset $S$ is defined as \begin{equation}\label{def:vol:subset} \vol_G(S) = \sum_{v \in S} d_G(v),\end{equation} and the cutset of $S$ is defined as \begin{equation}\label{def:cutset:subset} \cut_G(S, S^c) = \{ e \in E(G) \mid \text{$e$ has one end in $S$ and the other end in $S^c$} \}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-080: \begin{equation}\label{def:cutset:subset} \cut_G(S, S^c) = \{ e \in E(G) \mid $e$ has one end in $S$ and the other end in $S^c$ \}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1509: For every $\varepsilon > 0$, we define the edge-expander constant of the graph $G$ as \begin{equation}\label{def:expander-constant:graph} \alpha(G, \varepsilon) = \min_{\substack{S \subset V(G) \varepsilon |V(G)| \leq |S| \leq |V(G)|/2}} \frac{|\cut_G(S, S^c)|}{|S|}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1510: We call a graph $G$ an $(\alpha, \varepsilon, d)$-large-set expander if the graph has an average degree bounded above by $d$ and $\alpha(G, \varepsilon) \geq \alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-1511: A sequence of (possibly random) graphs $(G_n)_{n \geq 1}$ is called a large-set expander sequence with bounded average degree if there exists a $d < \infty$, and for every $\varepsilon \in (0,1/2)$, there exists $\alpha > 0$ such that \begin{equation}\label{def:large-set-expander-sequence} \prob(G_n \text{ is an } (\alpha, \varepsilon, d)\text{-large-set expander}) \to 1 \text{ as } n \to \infty.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-082: \begin{equation}\label{def:large-set-expander-sequence} \prob(G_n  is an  (\alpha, \varepsilon, d)-large-set expander) \to 1  as  n \to \infty.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-1512: Alimohammadi, Borgs, and Saberi \cite{ABS22 studied percolation on such sequences of large-set expanders with bounded average degree.}
elements.sent-2411-1513: Let $(G_n)_{n \geq 1}$ be a (possibly random) sequence of large-set expanders with bounded average degree, and let $\mu$ be a probability distribution on $\mathcal{G _\star$.}
elements.sent-2411-1514: For any graph $G \in \mathcal{G _\star$, we use $G(\pi)$ to denote the subgraph of $G$ obtained after performing bond percolation on $G$ with percolation probability $\pi$.}
elements.sent-2411-1515: Suppose $(G_n)_{n \geq 1}$ converges locally to $(G, o) \in \mathcal{G _\star$ with law $\mu$.}
elements.sent-2411-1516: We define $\Ccal_i^{\sss(n) (\pi)$ as the $i$-th largest component of the graph $G_n(\pi)$ (breaking ties arbitrarily) and $\mathscr{C (\pi)$ as the cluster containing the vertex $o$ in $G(\pi)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-1518: According to \cite[Theorem~1.1]{ABS22 , if $\pi \mapsto \zeta(\pi)$ is continuous at all $\pi \in [0,1]$, then \begin{equation}\label{expander:percolation-result:ABS22} \frac{|\Ccal_1^{\sss(n)}(\pi)|}{n} \overset{\prob}{\to} \zeta(\pi),\end{equation} where $\overset{\prob {\to}$ denotes convergence in probability with respect to both the random graph and percolation.}}

elements.section-2411-021: Preferential attachment models are large-set expanders

elements.chapter-2411-005: Component sizes in the sub-critical regime
elements.sent-2411-1658: In this chapter, we study the sub-critical regime of preferential attachment models.
elements.sent-2411-1659: Unlike configuration model with power-law degrees, we observe that the preferential attachment graph with finite variance degrees exhibits a larger largest connected component in the sub-critical regime than its maximum degree.
elements.sent-2411-1660: In this ongoing work, we have established a lower bound on the size of the largest connected component by utilising the continuous-time branching process embedding of preferential attachment trees.
elements.sent-2411-1661: We also provide a simulation study to support our proof of this lower bound.. \end{minipage \end{center.}}
elements.sent-2411-1662: Sub-critical percolation for a sequence of random graphs refers to the percolation regime when the edge-retention probability is below the critical percolation threshold.
elements.sent-2411-1663: In this phase, the graph typically consists of small, isolated sub-linear clusters, and the probability of finding a giant component that spans a significant portion of the graph tends to zero.
elements.sent-2411-1664: The study of sub-critical percolation is crucial for understanding the transition to the critical and super-critical phases, where the largest connected component gradually grows from a sub-linear to a linear scale, see for example \cite{B01,JLR00,BDvdHS20,DvdHvLS17 .}
elements.sent-2411-1665: This concept also has applications in various fields, such as network resilience in \cite{KY08,LLMSSG22 , where sub-critical percolation models the behaviour of a network under random failures.}
elements.sent-2411-1666: In Chapter~\ref{chap:local-giant , we explicitly computed the critical percolation threshold for a sequence of preferential attachment models with parameters \(m \geq 2\) and \(\delta>-m\).}
elements.sent-2411-1667: For non-positive \(\delta\), the critical percolation threshold is zero, whereas for positive \(\delta\), the critical percolation threshold is \(1/r(\mathbf{T _\kappa)\), as defined in Theorem~\ref{thm:main:theorem .}}
elements.sent-2411-1668: In this chapter, we analyse the size of the largest connected component in percolated preferential attachment models with a percolation probability less than the critical percolation threshold.
elements.sent-2411-1669: In \cite{B01 , Bollobás demonstrated that in an Erd\H{o s-R\'{e nyi random graph, the size of the largest connected component in the sub-critical regime is \( \Ocal(\log n) \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-1670: In \cite{D07 , Durrett observed that this behaviour does not hold when the underlying graph has a power-law degree distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-1671: In fact, the largest degree in such graphs is of the order \( n^{1/(\tau-1) \), where the empirical degree distribution follows a power law with exponent \( \tau \).}
elements.sent-2411-1672: Consequently, the size of the largest connected component in such graphs is at least of the order of the maximum degree.
elements.sent-2411-1673: Durrett conjectured in \cite[Conjecture~3.3.1]{D07 that, for a specific class of random graph models with a power-law degree distribution, \( n^{1/(\tau-1) \) represents the correct order of the largest connected component.}}
elements.sent-2411-1674: Janson \cite{J09 demonstrated that in configuration models with a given degree sequence following a power-law distribution with exponent \( \tau > 3 \), the size of the largest connected component is of the order \( n^{1/(\tau-1) \) in the sub-critical percolation regime.}}
elements.sent-2411-1675: Pittel \cite{P08 showed that a similar behaviour can be observed in the largest connected component of the percolated configuration model with a subpower-law degree distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-1676: For the preferential attachment models discussed in \cite{DM13 , near-critical behaviour was studied in \cite{EMO21 , where it was shown that, in the finite-variance regime of the asymptotic degree distribution, the size of the giant component decays exponentially as one approaches the critical edge density.}}
elements.sent-2411-1677: However, the study of the sub-critical phase in preferential attachment models, described in Chapter~\ref{chap:local-giant:sec:intro , has been much less explored.}
elements.sent-2411-1678: Unlike other power-law random graphs discussed earlier, preferential attachment models exhibit different behaviour in the sub-critical regime.
elements.sent-2411-1679: In this regime, we have shown that the size of the largest connected component in a preferential attachment model (d) is significantly larger than \( n^{1/(\tau-1) \), where \( \tau = 3 + \delta/m \).}
elements.sent-2411-1680: Although the exact exponent is not yet fully understood, simulation studies support our finding of a larger largest connected component.
elements.sent-2411-1681: In Section~\ref{sec:lower:bound , prove that the size of the largest connected component has larger exponent than that of the maximum degree in sub-critical preferential attachment models.}
elements.sent-2411-1682: In Section~\ref{sec:simulation , we provide some simulation study done to support our claim.}
elements.sent-2411-1683: Let \(D^{(n) (m,\delta)\) denote the maximal degree of a preferential attachment model of size \(n\) with parameters \(m \geq 1\) and \(\delta > -m\).}
elements.sent-2411-1685: In \cite[Theorem~8.14]{vdH1 , it is proven that \(D^{(n) (m,\delta)\) satisfies \eqn{ D^{(n) (m,\delta) n^{\chi-1}\overset{a.s.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1686: The following theorem establishes that the connected component of a preferential attachment tree containing an initial vertex has a size greater than \( n^{1-\chi \).}
elements.sent-2411-1687: For preferential attachment trees, the size of the largest connected component is of the order \(\Wcal n^{1-\chi+\pi\chi+o(1)}\), where \(\Wcal\) is a finite, almost surely positive, random variable.
elements.sent-2411-1688: To prove the theorem in the tree case, we utilise the continuous-time branching process (CTBP) representation of preferential attachment models, a technique introduced in \cite{AGS08 .}
elements.sent-2411-1689: By leveraging this representation, we show that for any \( \pi \in (0,1) \), the connected component containing vertex \(1\) has a size of order \( n^{1-\chi+\pi\chi \).}
elements.sent-2411-1690: For the CTBP representation of model~(d) with parameter \(\delta\), we consider the rate function \(f(\ell) = \ell + \delta + 1\) for \(\ell \in \mathbb{N \cup \{0\}\).}
elements.sent-2411-1691: We start with an initial graph of size \(2\), consisting of an edge connecting two vertices.
elements.sent-2411-1692: Let \(\sigma_\omega\) denote the birth time of vertex \(\omega\) in the CTBP, and \(\Pi_{\omega [0,t]\) denote the number of children of \(\omega\) at time \(t > 0\) after its birth at \(\sigma_\omega\).}
elements.sent-2411-1693: Conditionally on \(\Pi_{\omega [0,t] = \ell\), the birth time of the \((\ell + 1)\)-st offspring of \(\omega\) follows an exponential distribution with rate \(f(\ell)\).}
elements.sent-2411-1694: All these offspring then initiate their own birth processes with the same rate function.
elements.sent-2411-1695: The birth process for vertex \(1\) is slightly different with respect to the rate function.
elements.sent-2411-1696: Conditionally on \(\Pi_1[0,t] = \ell\), the birth time of the \((\ell + 1)\)-st offspring has an exponential distribution with rate \(f(\ell) - 1\).
elements.sent-2411-1697: The offspring of vertex \(1\) then start their own birth processes as described earlier, using the rate function \(f\).
elements.sent-2411-1698: Let \((T^{\sss (d) (t))_{t \geq 0}\) denote the resulting tree, and $Z(t)$ denote the size of \(T^{\sss (d) (t)\).}}
elements.sent-2411-1699: Define a stopping time \begin{equation}\tau_n = \inf \{t \geq 0 : Z(t) \geq n\}~,\end{equation} where \(\tau_n\) represents the time at which the CTBP reaches size \(n\).
elements.sent-2411-1700: We now proceed to compute the Malthusian parameter for this birth process.
elements.sent-2411-1701: For any $\lambda \geq 0$, define \eqn{ \phi(\lambda)=\E\Big[ \int\limits_0^\infty \e^{-\lambda t}\Pi_1(\,dt) \Big]=\E\Big[ \sum\limits_{k=1 ^\infty \e^{-\lambda\gamma_k \Big]~, where $\gamma_k$ is the birth time of the $k$-th child of vertex \(1\) in our birth process.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1702: As mentioned in \cite[Section~3]{BDO23 , the Malthusian parameter for the CTBP is defined as the unique solution to $\phi(\lambda)=1$.}
elements.sent-2411-1703: The Malthusian parameter for \( (T^{\sss (d) _{\pi (t))_{t \geq 0 \) is given by \eqn{ \lambda_{\pi =1+\pi(1+{\delta )~.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1704: Upon percolating \((T^{\sss (d) (t))_{t\geq 0}\) with probability $\pi \in (0,1]$, let \(\gamma_k(\pi)\) denote the birth time of the $k$-th child of vertex $1$ in \((T^{\sss (d) _{\pi (t))_{t\geq 0}\).}}}
elements.sent-2411-1705: Then, \eqn{ \gamma_k(\pi)=\sum\limits_{i=1 ^{N(k) E_i~, where $N(k)$ follows a negative-binomial distribution with parameters \(\pi\) and \(k\).}}}
elements.sent-2411-1706: Therefore, to obtain the Malthusian parameter of \((T^{\sss (d) _{\pi (t))_{t\geq 0}\), define \eqn{ \phi(\lambda,\pi)=\sum\limits_{k=1 ^\infty \E\Big[ \e^{-\lambda\gamma_k(\pi) \Big]~.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1707: Recall that in \((T^{\sss (d) _{\pi (t))_{t\geq 0}\), we percolate each edge independently with probability \(\pi\).}}
elements.sent-2411-1708: Therefore, \(\phi(\lambda,\pi)\) can be alternatively written as \eqan{ \phi(\lambda,\pi)= \E\Big[ \sum\limits_{k=1 ^{\infty B_i\e^{-\lambda\gamma_k \Big]~, where \((B_i)_{i\geq 1}\) is a sequence of i.i.d.\ ${\rm Bernoulli (\pi)$ random variables, and independent of \((\gamma_k)_{k\geq 1}\).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1709: Since \(E_i\) are independent exponential random variables, we simplify $\phi(\lambda,\pi)$ as \eqan{ \phi(\lambda,\pi)= \E\Big[ \sum\limits_{k=1 ^\infty B_i\e^{-\lambda\gamma_k \Big]\nn = \sum\limits_{k=1 ^{\infty}\E[B_k]\E[\e^{-\lambda\gamma_k ]\nn = \pi\sum\limits_{\ell=1 ^\infty \frac{\Gamma(\ell+1+\delta) {\Gamma(1+\delta)}\frac{\Gamma(1+\delta+\lambda) {\Gamma(\ell+1+\delta+\lambda) ~.}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-1735: We conducted a simulation study to support our theorem for preferential attachment trees.
elements.sent-2411-1736: In the simulation, we compared the size of the largest connected component in sub-critical preferential attachment models with the maximum degree in the non-percolated graph.
elements.sent-2411-1737: On the other hand, in Theorem~\ref{thm:lower-bound , we proved that for preferential attachment models, the size of the largest connected component satisfies \begin{equation}|\Ccal^{(n)}_1(p)| = \Wcal n^{1-\chi+\pi\chi+o(1)}~for m=1~, and\quad|\Ccal^{(n)}_1(p)| \geq \Wcal n^{1-\chi+\pi\chi+o(1)}~for m>1~. \label{eq:pLCC:1}\end{equation} We plotted \(\log |\Ccal^{(n) _1(p)|\) and \(\log D^{\sss(n)}\) against \(\log n\).}}
elements.sent-2411-1738: We then fitted straight lines and computed the slope of each fitted line.
elements.sent-2411-1739: To reduce variation in the slope due to the effect of the random variables \(\Wcal\) and \(W_\infty\), we computed their difference and compared it with the theoretical value \(\chi \pi\) observed in \eqref{eq:pLCC:1 .}
elements.sent-2411-1740: We compiled the simulation results for several values of \(\chi\) and \(\pi\) in this section.
elements.sent-2411-1741: We defined the following sequence of integers to represent the graph sizes: \eqn{ {\bf N = \{ x \cdot 10^y : x \in [9],~\text{and y \in \{2,3,4 \}\}.}}}

elements.sent-2411-1743: For preferential attachment graphs with \(m=2\), we examined two percolation probabilities \(0.85\pi_c\) and \(0.9\pi_c\), where \(\pi_c\) is the critical percolation threshold.
elements.sent-2411-1744: Probabilities below \(0.8\pi_c\) were not simulated, as they approach zero and could introduce numerical errors.
elements.sent-2411-1745: For each combination of \(m\), \(\delta\), percolation probability \(\pi\), and \(n \in {\bf N}\), we simulated 200 preferential attachment models of size \(n\).
elements.sent-2411-1746: We percolated each graph with probability \(\pi\) and computed the maximum degree and the size of the largest connected component.

elements.sent-2411-1761: In this chapter, we study the Ising model on preferential attachment models.
elements.sent-2411-1762: Building on the work of Dembo, Montanari, and Sun (2013), we compute the explicit thermodynamic limit of pressure per particle by utilising the local limit of preferential attachment models.
elements.sent-2411-1763: We use the convexity properties of the internal energy and magnetisation to determine their thermodynamic limits as the size of the graph tends to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-1764: To identify the inverse critical temperature, we prove that the inverse critical temperature for a sequence of graphs and its local limit are equal.
elements.sent-2411-1765: Finally, we show that $\beta_c(m,\delta)$ is the inverse critical temperature for the P\'{o lya point tree with parameters $m$ and $\delta$, using results from Lyons.}
elements.sent-2411-1766: This part of the proof heavily relies on the critical percolation threshold for P\'{o lya point trees established earlier.. \end{minipage \end{center.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1767: The Ising model, originally introduced to describe ferromagnetism in statistical mechanics, represents a system of spins on a lattice, where each spin can take one of two states.
elements.sent-2411-1768: The interactions between neighbouring spins determine the system's overall magnetic properties.
elements.sent-2411-1769: This model has since found applications beyond physics, including in sociology, biology, and finance, where it helps to explain the collective behaviour of interconnected entities.
elements.sent-2411-1770: The Ising model has been extensively studied in the literature; see, for example, \cite{G06 .}
elements.sent-2411-1771: Recently, the focus in this field has extended to the study of the Ising model on random graphs.
elements.sent-2411-1772: Dembo and Montanari studied the Ising model on locally tree-like graphs in \cite{DM10 , restricting their analysis to cases where the average degree has finite variance.}
elements.sent-2411-1773: Under certain continuity assumptions, the authors examined the Ising measure on uniformly sparse locally tree-like graphs.
elements.sent-2411-1774: The Ising model on Erd\H{o s-Renyi graphs in both the zero and high-temperature regimes was studied in \cite{GdS08 .}}
elements.sent-2411-1775: The Ising model on configuration models has been analysed in \cite{DM10,DGvdH10 .}
elements.sent-2411-1776: Dembo, Montanari and Sun also studied factor models on locally tree-like graphs, shedding light on the Ising model in these contexts in \cite{DMS13 .}
elements.sent-2411-1777: In Section~\ref{sec:preliminary:results , we describe some frequently used lemma that we use in this chapter without proof.}
elements.sent-2411-1778: Proofs to the lemmata described in this section could be obtained in the references provided following the statements of the lemma.
elements.sent-2411-1779: In Section~\ref{sec:thermodynamic:limit , we prove thermodynamic limits of several thermodynamic quantities.}
elements.sent-2411-1780: Lastly, in Section~\ref{sec:inv:critical-temperature , we compute the inverse critical temperature for preferential attachment models.}

elements.sent-2411-1903: In this section, we state and prove the thermodynamic limits of two thermodynamic quantities, namely internal energy and magnetisation for the Ising model on preferential attachment models: \begin{Theorem [Thermodynamic quantities] Let $\{G_n\}_{n\geq 1}$ be a sequence of preferential attachment graphs with parameters $m$ and $\delta$.}
elements.sent-2411-1905: Let $M_n(\beta,B)=\frac{1 {n}\langle\sum_{i\in[n]}\sigma_i\rangle_{\mu_n}$ be the magnetisation per vertex.}
elements.sent-2411-1906: Then, its thermodynamic limit exists and is given by \eqn{ M_n(\beta,B)\overset{\prob {\to M(\beta,B)=\frac{\partial {\partial B}\varphi(\beta,B)=\E[\tanh(\h(\emp))]~.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1907: Let $U_n(\beta,B)=-\frac{1 {n \langle\sum_{(u,v)\in E_n \sigma_u\sigma_v\rangle_{\mu_n}$ denote the internal energy per vertex.}}}
elements.sent-2411-1908: Then, its thermodynamic limit exists and is given by \eqan{ U_n(\beta,B)\overset{\prob {\to U(\beta,B)= -\frac{\partial {\partial\beta}\varphi(\beta,B) = -m\E\left[ \frac{\tanh(\beta)+\tanh(\h(\hat{\emp ))\tanh(\h(\hat{\emp 1)) {1+\tanh(\beta)\tanh(\h(\hat{\emp ))\tanh(\h(\hat{\emp 1)) \right]~.\nn.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1909: To prove this theorem, we utilise the fact that $\varphi(\beta, B)$ is a convex function with respect to both $\beta$ and $B$.
elements.sent-2411-1910: The following lemma establishes this convexity property of $\varphi$: \begin{Lemma [Convexity of $\psi_n(\beta, B)$] For any $\beta \in\R$ and $B \in \mathbb{R}$, let $\psi_n(\beta, B)$ be as defined in Theorem~\ref{thm:thermodynamic-limit:pressure .}}
elements.sent-2411-1911: Then, both $\beta \mapsto \psi_n(\beta, B)$ and $B \mapsto \psi_n(\beta, B)$ are convex functions of $\beta$ and $B$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-1912: This lemma relies on the fact that preferential attachment models locally converge in probability to the P\'{o lya point tree.}
elements.sent-2411-1913: Then, \begin{equation}\frac{1}{|E_n|}\sum_{\{i, j\} \in E_n}\langle \sigma_i \sigma_j \rangle_{\mu_n} \overset{\prob}{\to} \E\left[ \frac{\tanh(\beta) + \tanh(\h(\hat{\emp})) \tanh(\h(\hat{\emp }1))}{1 + \tanh(\beta) \tanh(\h(\hat{\emp})) \tanh(\h(\hat{\emp }1))} \right]~, \label{eq:lem:limiting:edge-measure-1} and \qquad \frac{1}{n}\sum_{v \in [n]}\langle \sigma_v \rangle_{\mu_n} \overset{\prob}{\to} \E[\tanh(\h(\emp))]~. \label{eq:lem:limiting:edge-measure-2}\end{equation} \end{Lemma With Lemmas~\ref{lem:convexity:pressure-limit and \ref{lem:limiting:edge-measure in place, we now proceed to prove Theorem~\ref{thm:limit:thermodynamic-quantities :.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1914: To prove the convergence part, we use the convexity argument from Lemma~\ref{lem:convexity:pressure-limit .}
elements.sent-2411-1915: Since $\beta \mapsto \psi_n(\beta, B)$ is a convex function in $\beta$, for any $\vep > 0$, \begin{equation}\label{for:prop:limiting:ie:01} \frac{1}{\vep}\left[ \psi_n(\beta, B) - \psi_n(\beta - \vep, B) \right] \leq \frac{\partial}{\partial \beta} \psi_n(\beta, B) \nn \leq \frac{1}{\vep}\left[ \psi_n(\beta + \vep, B) - \psi_n(\beta, B) \right]~.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-1916: Taking limits as $n \to \infty$ in \eqref{for:prop:limiting:ie:01 , and using Theorem~\ref{thm:thermodynamic-limit:pressure , we obtain, for any $\vep > 0$, \begin{equation}\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{\vep} \left[ \psi_n(\beta, B) - \psi_n(\beta - \vep, B) \right] \overset{\prob}{\to} \frac{1}{\vep} \left[ \varphi(\beta, B) - \varphi(\beta - \vep, B) \right], \label{for:prop:limiting:ie:02} and~ \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{\vep} \left[ \psi_n(\beta + \vep, B) - \psi_n(\beta, B) \right] \overset{\prob}{\to} \frac{1}{\vep} \left[ \varphi(\beta + \vep, B) - \varphi(\beta, B) \right]. \label{for:prop:limiting:ie:03}\end{equation} Since the inequality in \eqref{for:prop:limiting:ie:01 holds for any $\vep > 0$, we take the limit $\vep \to 0$ to obtain $\frac{\partial {\partial \beta \varphi(\beta, B)$ as the limit of the right-hand sides of both \eqref{for:prop:limiting:ie:02 and \eqref{for:prop:limiting:ie:03 .}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-2012: Research in mathematics, rather than narrowing the scope of open questions, tends to exponentially increase them.
elements.sent-2411-2013: Our work on preferential attachment models follows this trend, revealing a range of new research directions in the field.
elements.sent-2411-2014: Throughout this thesis, we have explored the dynamics of preferential attachment models, focusing on both graph dynamics within these models and the effects of different settings.
elements.sent-2411-2015: In Part I, we examined how i.i.d.\ out-edge settings influence the dynamics of these models and their impact on the local limit.
elements.sent-2411-2016: Although we successfully established the local limit for the generalised models, our study of stochastic processes in Part II is limited to deterministic out-edge settings.
elements.sent-2411-2017: Consequently, we believe that a vast array of open questions remains to be explored, presenting a rich landscape for future research.
elements.sent-2411-2018: In this section, we try to put up some of these open directions.
elements.sent-2411-2019: We divide these in two directions: local limit direction and stochastic processes direction.
elements.sent-2411-2020: Lo studied the case of the preferential attachment tree, where each new vertex joins the graph with an i.i.d.\ random fitness parameter in \cite{tiffany2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-2021: The author suggested that this result could be generalised to the fixed out-edge preferential attachment graph case as well.
elements.sent-2411-2022: This finding is particularly interesting as it provides the rate of convergence in the total variation metric.
elements.sent-2411-2023: It would therefore be intriguing to extend this work to random fitness distributions, along with random out-edge distributions and to study the rate of convergence of these generalised preferential attachment models to the local limit, some further generalised version of the random P\'{o lya point tree.}
elements.sent-2411-2024: In Chapter~\ref{chap:percolation_threshold_PPT , we explicitly computed the critical percolation threshold for the P\'{o lya point tree with parameters \( m\geq 2\) and \(\delta>0\).}}
elements.sent-2411-2025: The computation of the spectral radius of the mean offspring operator for the P\'{o lya point tree can also be extended to the random P\'{o lya point tree.}}
elements.sent-2411-2026: Investigating the critical percolation threshold for the random P\'{o lya point tree using the spectral radius of its mean offspring operator would be very interesting.}
elements.sent-2411-2027: The main challenge in this computation lies in proving the Kesten-Stigum condition for super-criticality.
elements.sent-2411-2028: Furthermore, we hypothesise that the critical percolation threshold for the random P\'{o lya point tree is \(0\) when $\delta$ is non-positive.}
elements.sent-2411-2029: We also believe that our proof in Chapter~\ref{chap:local-giant can be extended to other preferential attachment models, such as the independent models.}
elements.sent-2411-2030: For instance, a similar technique may prove useful in identifying critical percolation thresholds for preferential attachment models with i.i.d.\ random out-edges, as studied in \cite{DEH09,GV17,RRR22 .}
elements.sent-2411-2031: Our proof of the large-set expander property for the preferential attachment model extensively relies on the fact that \(m\) is fixed and at least 2.
elements.sent-2411-2032: Therefore, subject to the critical percolation threshold of the random P\'{o lya point tree, the main challenge in determining the critical percolation threshold for generalised preferential attachment models lies in proving the large-set expander property of these models.}
elements.sent-2411-2033: Although we have identified the exact critical percolation thresholds for several preferential attachment models, our techniques do not reveal the order of the phase transition.
elements.sent-2411-2034: Alimohammadi, Borgs, and Saberi have proved that this phase transition is of infinite order for \(\delta=0\), i.e.\ \(\zeta(\pi)\) is infinitely differentiable at \(\pi_c\).
elements.sent-2411-2035: Eckhoff, M\"orters, and Ortgiese proved in \cite{EMO21 that the phase transition in Bernoulli preferential attachment models defined in \cite{DM13 is also of infinite order.}}
elements.sent-2411-2036: Thus, we conjecture that the phase transition is of infinite order for other preferential attachment models and any admissible \(\delta\).
elements.sent-2411-2037: In Chapter~\ref{chap:subcritical , we discussed our recent ongoing work on the sub-critical percolation regime of preferential attachment models, where we observe a very interesting behaviour in the sub-critical largest connected component.}
elements.sent-2411-2038: Unlike the typical behaviour in other random graphs with power-law degree distributions, such as configuration models, the sub-critical largest connected component in preferential attachment models is significantly larger than its maximum degree.
elements.sent-2411-2039: It would be interesting to study the critical regime of preferential attachment models, as we believe that some intriguing phenomena may also emerge within the critical window of these models.
elements.sent-2411-2040: We studied the Ising model in the quenched setting of preferential attachment models and identified the inverse critical temperature for these models.
elements.sent-2411-2041: However, we could not determine the order of the phase transition in this setting.
elements.sent-2411-2042: Moreover, the annealed setting on preferential attachment models would also be of significant interest.
elements.sent-2411-2043: From the work of Dembo, Montanari, and Sun \cite{DMS13 , we gain insights into the thermodynamic limits of the pressure per particle in various factor models on locally tree-like graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-2044: Consequently, the study of Potts models on preferential attachment models, in both quenched and annealed settings, would be highly intriguing for the statistical physics community.
elements.sent-2411-2045: Summary In real life, networks are dynamic in nature; they grow over time and often exhibit power-law degree sequences.
elements.sent-2411-2046: To model the evolving structure of the internet, Barab\'{a si and Albert introduced a simple dynamic model with a power-law degree distribution.}
elements.sent-2411-2047: This model has since been generalised, giving rise to a large class of affine preferential attachment models, where each new vertex connects to existing vertices with a probability proportional to the current degree of the vertex.
elements.sent-2411-2048: While several studies have analysed global and local properties of these random graphs, their dynamic nature and the dependencies in edge-connection probabilities have posed significant analytical challenges.
elements.sent-2411-2049: In this thesis, we explore various properties of these dynamic random graphs in a broad context.
elements.sent-2411-2050: First, we extend the preferential attachment models to allow each new vertex to connect with a random number of initial neighbours.
elements.sent-2411-2051: To understand the local structure of these generalised preferential attachment models, we aim to identify their local limits as described by Benjamini and Schramm in \(2001\).
elements.sent-2411-2052: In \(2014\), Berger et al.\ proved that the standard preferential attachment models are locally tree-like, meaning that the finite neighbourhood of a uniformly chosen vertex resembles a branching process, termed the P\'{o lya point tree.}
elements.sent-2411-2053: We demonstrate that, even after generalisation, preferential attachment models retain this tree-like property.
elements.sent-2411-2054: The finite neighbourhood of a uniformly chosen vertex in these graphs resembles another branching process, which we name the generalised P\'{o lya point tree.}
elements.sent-2411-2055: Additionally, we prove that, despite the strong dependencies in edge connections, certain generalised preferential attachment models exhibit conditional independence.
elements.sent-2411-2056: This result facilitates a more detailed analysis of these models.
elements.sent-2411-2057: The second part of this thesis focuses on stochastic processes on preferential attachment models.
elements.sent-2411-2058: These stochastic processes introduce an additional layer of randomness to the random graphs.
elements.sent-2411-2059: Examples include bond and site percolation, random walks, the Ising and Potts models, and Gaussian processes on random graphs.
elements.sent-2411-2060: While these processes are global properties of the graphs and often require more than just local behaviour known from the local limit, under certain conditions, they can be analysed using only local properties.
elements.sent-2411-2061: For instance, in vertex-transitive graph sequences, bond percolation can be studied using local properties alone.
elements.sent-2411-2062: In this thesis, we specifically examine bond percolation and the Ising model on preferential attachment models.
elements.sent-2411-2063: We identify the critical probability \(\pi_c\) such that when the model is percolated with a probability higher than \(\pi_c\), a single largest connected component of the order of the graph's size emerges.
elements.sent-2411-2064: Conversely, for percolation probabilities below \(\pi_c\), no such large connected component forms.
elements.sent-2411-2065: We show that this property can indeed be studied using the local properties of the preferential attachment models.
elements.sent-2411-2066: We further investigate the size of the largest connected component when the graph is percolated with a probability below \(\pi_c\), uncovering surprising results compared to similar static random graphs with power-law degree distributions.
elements.sent-2411-2067: Finally, we study the Ising ferromagnetic model on preferential attachment models.
elements.sent-2411-2068: Building on the work of Dembo, Montanari, and Sun (\(2014\)) and Lyons (\(1990\)), we demonstrate that various thermodynamic quantities of interest in the Ising model can also be analysed using the local limit of the graph sequence.
elements.sent-2411-2069: Our study indicates that while preferential attachment models satisfy most of the regularity conditions necessary for examining stochastic processes through local limits, their dynamic nature and the strong dependencies in edge-connection probabilities may lead to unexpected results for certain stochastic processes on these models.. \pagestyle{empty \cleardoublepage\null\cleardoublepage.}
